one_liner: Every Pedigree ad dog is a real shelter dog nearby.
search_doc:
Every Pedigree ad dog is a real shelter dog nearby.
Mechanisms: media-as-product, ai-powered-personalization, geolocation-targeting, purpose-driven-product
Idea: Partnering with Nexus Studios, we developed a bespoke AI system that can transform a single image of a shelter dog into studio-quality photography. This 'glow-up' image is then mapped to a CGI rig, allowing the photo-real double to be positioned in any pose to suit any piece of PEDIGREE creative. Now, every dog we feature in our digital advertising can be a real shelter dog near you. Adoptable uses geolocation data to place the ads in neighbourhoods and locations that suit the individual dogs best. And when that dog is adopted it's instantly dropped out of media rotation. Adoptable supercharges PEDIGREE's purpose of ending dog homelessness. Because now, instead of making an ad campaign about adoption, we can make every ad Adoptable.
Insight: Shelter dogs are overlooked because amateur shelter photography cannot compete with polished brand imagery — but if a shelter dog can be shown in Pedigree-quality imagery, more people will adopt.

extracted.tagline: Turning every PEDIGREE ad into an ad for a shelter dog.
extracted.context: Every PEDIGREE ad always features a dog in it. We wondered, what if that dog could be Adoptable.
extracted.idea: Partnering with Nexus Studios, we developed a bespoke AI system that can transform a single image of a shelter dog into studio-quality photography. This 'glow-up' image is then mapped to a CGI rig, allowing the photo-real double to be positioned in any pose to suit any piece of PEDIGREE creative. Now, every dog we feature in our digital advertising can be a real shelter dog near you. Adoptable uses geolocation data to place the ads in neighbourhoods and locations that suit the individual dogs best. And when that dog is adopted it's instantly dropped out of media rotation. Adoptable supercharges PEDIGREE's purpose of ending dog homelessness. Because now, instead of making an ad campaign about adoption, we can make every ad Adoptable.
execution: AI pipeline turns a single shelter-dog photo into studio-grade imagery
CGI rig re-poses the same dog into any Pedigree creative
Geo-targeted digital OOH placed near each dog's actual shelter
QR code on each ad opens the dog's adoption profile
Ad automatically removed from rotation once the dog is adopted
metrics: 50% of dogs adopted in first 2 weeks

inferred.insight: Shelter dogs are overlooked because amateur shelter photography cannot compete with polished brand imagery — but if a shelter dog can be shown in Pedigree-quality imagery, more people will adopt.
inferred.one_liner: Every Pedigree ad dog is a real shelter dog nearby.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Rewires the brand's entire digital media spend into an adoption engine: each impression doubles as a working ad for a specific, local, adoptable dog, with 6x more shelter site visits and 50% of featured dogs adopted within two weeks.

visual.style_description: Hero portrait of a single black-and-white shelter dog standing on a clean white background; left column carries the campaign name and explanatory body text in editorial typography; right side shows a strip of yellow Pedigree DOOH placements in real urban environments and a QR-code adoption-profile mockup.

idea.rationale: Reframing the brand's digital media buy as a live adoption product is a strong idea, with each impression tied to a specific, local, adoptable dog that disappears the moment she finds a home. In a Creative Business Transformation context, however, this is a media-system pivot rather than a new product, service or P&L change — it scores high on originality but partially on category-native fit.

strategy.rationale: Pedigree is Mars's global dog-food brand whose long-running 'Feed the Good' / 'Dogs Rule' platform has anchored shelter-adoption advocacy for years, so committing the media buy to adoption is exact mission-fit, not borrowed purpose. The insight — that shelter dogs are overlooked because amateur photography cannot compete with polished brand imagery — is a plausible cultural truth the AI 'glow-up' directly attacks. The board states the insight as self-evident rather than backing it with adoption-conversion data, and the business diagnosis for Pedigree itself (why this shifts dog-food sales or share) is not articulated, which is the Strategy soft spot in a business-transformation frame.

execution.rationale: The bespoke AI restoration + CGI repose pipeline allows a single shelter snapshot to be re-posed into any Pedigree creative, with geo-targeted DOOH placed near each dog's actual shelter, QR-to-adopt handoff and auto-removal from rotation on adoption. The orchestration closes a real loop from billboard to shelter to adoption and the yellow-poster system keeps coherence across hundreds of unique dogs.

impact.rationale: The headline '50% of featured dogs adopted in first 2 weeks' is the right behaviour-change outcome but the cohort size is not disclosed — '50% of how many dogs?' remains open, which limits how heavily Impact can lean on it. The '6x more shelter site visits' is a credible directional proxy but is also entrant-reported and unanchored to an absolute baseline. The '100% purpose-led media investment by 2026' is a commitment rather than a result, and no Pedigree business outcome (sales, share, household penetration) is shown — in a Creative Business Transformation context the missing business-side proof is the load-bearing gap.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver here rather than the Grand Prix it took in Outdoor because, in a Creative Business Transformation frame, this is a media-buy pivot with strong adoption proxies but no disclosed cohort base and no disclosed Pedigree business outcome.

one_liner: A beer brand restores Inca water canals against drought.
search_doc:
A beer brand restores Inca water canals against drought.
Mechanisms: ancestral-technology-revival, purpose-driven-infrastructure, brand-as-environmental-actor, documentary-as-campaign
Idea: Resurrect the Inca technology called Amunas, stone and clay channels that carry rainwater to capture and reinject it, passing through porous strata that filter and warm it to extend carrying capacity into the dry season. Reactivated as part of the campaign, the recovery is endorsed by UNESCO as an example of eco-hydrology.
Insight: Modern engineering keeps failing Andean water scarcity, while the answer — a 1,400-year-old Inca canal network — was buried under colonial history and waiting to be re-activated.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE BUSINESS TRANSFORMATION/BRONZE/Amunas Recovery - Cusquena (Backus - Ab Inbev, Lima _ Publicis Peru, Lima).jpg

extracted.tagline: How an ancestral technology from 1400 years ago is saving the lack of water in the present.
extracted.context: When the Spanish conquistadors arrived in Peru, they forgot to consider the country, erasing all evidence of the Inca Empire, including the most advanced system to bring water: the Amunas.
extracted.idea: Resurrect the Inca technology called Amunas, stone and clay channels that carry rainwater to capture and reinject it, passing through porous strata that filter and warm it to extend carrying capacity into the dry season. Reactivated as part of the campaign, the recovery is endorsed by UNESCO as an example of eco-hydrology.
execution: Restoration of Inca-era Amunas stone-and-clay water channels
Local communities trained to maintain the channels
UNESCO endorsement of the restored system as eco-hydrology
Documentary / film content rolled out across press
metrics: 15 million m³ of water potentially available

inferred.insight: Modern engineering keeps failing Andean water scarcity, while the answer — a 1,400-year-old Inca canal network — was buried under colonial history and waiting to be re-activated.
inferred.one_liner: A beer brand restores Inca water canals against drought.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Cusquena pivots brand investment into restoring pre-colonial Inca water-management infrastructure, with 67km of canals to be restored by 2025 and 15 million m³ of water unlocked, validated by UNESCO and amplified by global press.

visual.style_description: Dark, documentary-toned board: a long stone Amunas water channel snakes through the green Andean landscape as the hero image, framed by colonial-era portraits and archival imagery at the top corners; metric callouts and a dense logo row of UNESCO and global press anchor the bottom.

idea.rationale: Reactivating a 1,400-year-old Inca canal network as the central business asset of a beer brand is a distinctive idea — ancestral infrastructure repurposed as water-security investment. As a Creative Business Transformation case, it lives at the edge of native fit: it is closer to a long-term CSR / brand-purpose programme than a change to how Cusquena operates or generates revenue.

strategy.rationale: Cusquena is the Peruvian premium beer of Backus (AB InBev's Peruvian subsidiary), positioned around Inca / Andean cultural heritage, so funding the restoration of Inca water infrastructure is mission-coherent and not borrowed purpose. The water-scarcity context is real and verifiable through Peruvian and international water-stress data, though the board states the colonial-erasure framing in narrative rather than data terms. The link to Cusquena's actual business diagnosis — water dependency of beer brewing, supply-chain risk in the Andes, category share — is implied rather than stated, which is the soft spot in a business-transformation frame.

execution.rationale: The build combines physical restoration of stone-and-clay channels, training of local communities to maintain them, a UNESCO endorsement layer that converts the work from CSR to recognised eco-hydrology, and a documentary asset rolled out through global earned press. Channels are well-matched to a heritage-restoration story — long-form documentary plus international news titles (Washington Post, BBC, Forbes, Le Monde, Newsweek, AdAge) — though the board does not detail the partnership architecture or financing model behind the restoration itself.

impact.rationale: The headline numbers are forward-looking commitments rather than booked results: '67 kilometres will be restored by 2025' and '15 million m³ of water potentially available' are projections, not delivered outcomes, and 'potentially available' is a flagged hedge. The 2,600 animals protected figure is absolute and a credible directional proxy. The UNESCO endorsement is a real validation signal, but global press coverage is entrant-reported and unaudited. The Cusquena business outcome (sales, share, brand-equity lift in Peru) is not shown, which in a Creative Business Transformation context is the load-bearing absence.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the ancestral-infrastructure idea and UNESCO endorsement are genuinely distinctive, but the impact case rests on forward-looking projections and 'potentially available' water with no Cusquena business outcome disclosed.

one_liner: Free car during your job trial, paid only once you stay.
search_doc:
Free car during your job trial, paid only once you stay.
Mechanisms: business-model-pivot, financial-product-as-the-idea, purpose-driven-distribution, public-private-partnership
Idea: Renault Group provides cars for free to people during their job trial periods, that they only start to pay at affordable conditions once their job is secured. Cars made accessible to those who need them the most, when they need it the most. Cars to Work is available through 50 dealerships in mobility deserts and on FranceTravail.fr, the French public employment service.
Insight: Job-seekers in transport deserts are excluded from work because no bank will finance a car during a probation period — flipping that financing rule unlocks both employment and car-ownership.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE BUSINESS TRANSFORMATION/GOLD/Cars To Work - Renault (Publicis Conseil Paris).webp

extracted.tagline: No car, no job. No job, no loan. No loan, no car. — voitures de future fonction.
extracted.context: A vicious circle for society's most fragile, a creator of social exclusion: 40% of French people live in areas with no public transportation, places where a car is the only solution. 54% of French job-seekers have already turned down a position because they have no mobility solution. But often buying a car is not an option. In France, a new job starts with a three months trial period where you can be fired any time, so you are not eligible for a car loan.
extracted.idea: Renault Group provides cars for free to people during their job trial periods, that they only start to pay at affordable conditions once their job is secured. Cars made accessible to those who need them the most, when they need it the most. Cars to Work is available through 50 dealerships in mobility deserts and on FranceTravail.fr, the French public employment service.
execution: Free-during-trial-period car loan program for new hires
Affordable repayments triggered only once the job is confirmed
Distribution via 50 Cars to Work dealerships in mobility-desert regions
Integrated with FranceTravail.fr public employment service
press_coverage: "To benefit from this offer is a huge relief, it widens my opportunities." — Marie Toure, Cars to Work customer

inferred.insight: Job-seekers in transport deserts are excluded from work because no bank will finance a car during a probation period — flipping that financing rule unlocks both employment and car-ownership.
inferred.one_liner: Free car during your job trial, paid only once you stay.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Renault turns its dealer network into a social-mobility platform by financing cars only once a probation period converts into a job, reaching 8.8 million people through 50 dedicated dealerships and the French public employment service.

visual.style_description: Cinematic open-road hero shot of a white Renault driving away into a flat agricultural landscape; the headline 'cars to work' sits centred with a circling 'No car, no job, no loan' loop typography making the vicious-circle concept literal; a small France map and four execution thumbnails plus three large metric callouts anchor the bottom band.

idea.rationale: Inverting the standard car-loan logic — financing the car during the probation period and only billing once the job is secured — is a genuine financial-product invention, not an ad. The 'no car / no job / no loan' looping headline literalises a real circular trap, making the idea instantly legible and native to a business-transformation category.

strategy.rationale: Renault Group is the French national carmaker whose recent corporate repositioning around 'mobility for all' makes a dealer-network-as-social-platform play mission-coherent, not borrowed purpose. The two anchor data points are specific and verifiable in French public statistics — 40% of French people live in areas without public transport, and 54% of job-seekers have turned down a position because of mobility — and the insight that probation periods disqualify new hires from car finance is a real, observable structural barrier rather than an invented one.

execution.rationale: The build extends beyond a comms layer: 50 dedicated dealerships were stood up in mobility-desert regions, 6,000 cars were earmarked for the programme, and distribution was wired into FranceTravail.fr, the French public employment service, which gives the offer institutional reach rather than just a campaign URL. The orchestration combines owned dealer infrastructure, a public-private partnership and broad earned-media coverage across French national TV (TF1, M6, Canal+, arte) and press (Le Monde, Le Parisien, Ouest-France).

impact.rationale: The '8.8 million French people reached' figure is a reach claim, not a business outcome, and is entrant-reported rather than independently audited. The 50 dealerships and 6,000 cars are absolute operational numbers and the right kind of structural proof for a business-transformation case, though the board does not disclose how many trial periods converted into confirmed jobs and paid-back loans, which is the load-bearing outcome a social-finance pivot should ultimately demonstrate. A single customer quote (Marie Toure) anchors the human result but cannot stand in for cohort-level conversion data.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because Renault built a real social-finance product wired into the French public employment service, with hard operational numbers — held back from Grand Prix because the conversion outcome from trial period to confirmed job-and-loan is not disclosed.

one_liner: A celebrity tabloid quote becomes a real product.
search_doc:
A celebrity tabloid quote becomes a real product.
Mechanisms: real-time-product-launch, celebrity-news-hijack, name-as-content, sku-revival
Idea: In 24 hours, we relaunched Heinz's Ketchup and Seemingly Ranch, turning Heinz's worst-performing product into its most beloved. Just days later, with all the buzz from a real-life cultural moment, we listed bottles online and in 600 stores. The limited-edition limited bottles sold out in just 24 hours. Turning our limited-edition bottles into a permanent item nationwide. Spending $3 on partnerships, Heinz reached the world's most loyal foodservice consumers, driving the future of HEINZ sauce creation. Walmart continued to sell Heinz Ketchup & Seemingly Ranch nationally.
Insight: When a tabloid headline accidentally describes your dead product on the shelf, the headline IS the marketing brief — if you can ship in 24 hours.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE BUSINESS TRANSFORMATION/BRONZE/Heinz Ketchup & Seemingly RANCH - Heinz Ketchup (Rethink Toronto).jpg

extracted.tagline: In 24 hours, we transformed Heinz's worst seller into a best seller.
extracted.context: In 2019, Heinz launched Ranch — a mashup of ketchup and ranch. Despite leading in three categories in Heinz history, Ranch was its worst-performing product in close to a decade. Then, in 2023, while the internet obsessed over Taylor Swift's new relationship with NFL star Travis Kelce, a photo of her 'eating a piece of chicken with ketchup and seemingly ranch' went viral. We saw an opportunity.
extracted.idea: In 24 hours, we relaunched Heinz's Ketchup and Seemingly Ranch, turning Heinz's worst-performing product into its most beloved. Just days later, with all the buzz from a real-life cultural moment, we listed bottles online and in 600 stores. The limited-edition limited bottles sold out in just 24 hours. Turning our limited-edition bottles into a permanent item nationwide. Spending $3 on partnerships, Heinz reached the world's most loyal foodservice consumers, driving the future of HEINZ sauce creation. Walmart continued to sell Heinz Ketchup & Seemingly Ranch nationally.
execution: Limited-edition Ketchup & Seemingly Ranch bottle relaunch within 24 hours of the viral moment
Online sales plus listings in 600+ stores
Social-first PR campaign riding the Taylor Swift / Travis Kelce news cycle
Conversion of the limited edition into a permanent SKU at Walmart
metrics: 6 billion most-earned impressions in brand history
press_coverage: "No doubt they will sell out immediately."
"Heinz breathed life into an obscure condiment."
"A testament to the power of dip."

inferred.insight: When a tabloid headline accidentally describes your dead product on the shelf, the headline IS the marketing brief — if you can ship in 24 hours.
inferred.one_liner: A celebrity tabloid quote becomes a real product.
inferred.qualitative_summary: A dead Heinz SKU is resurrected in 24 hours by literalising a viral Taylor Swift / Travis Kelce tabloid line into a real bottle, selling 320% above forecast, driving +$160M in sales and the most earned impressions in brand history.

visual.style_description: Bright red poster-style board with the hero Heinz Ketchup & Seemingly Ranch bottle flanked by cut-out photos of Travis Kelce in his Chiefs jersey and Taylor Swift in concert, literalising the celebrity-origin story; press quotes run across the top in a tabloid-quote strip, with bold metric callouts on the right and a dense press logo wall at the bottom.

idea.rationale: Literalising a viral tabloid headline ('eating chicken with ketchup and seemingly ranch') into a real SKU in 24 hours is a sharp real-time-product idea and a clever SKU revival. As a Creative Business Transformation case it is narrower than the category usually rewards — a single-SKU relaunch riding a celebrity news cycle, not a business-model or operating-model change.

strategy.rationale: Heinz is Kraft Heinz's flagship condiment brand with global category leadership in ketchup, and reviving a moribund Ketchup-Ranch hybrid via a viral tabloid headline plays to the brand's well-established cultural-listening posture (e.g. prior 'Tip Jar', 'Draw Ketchup' work) — the idea is mission-coherent rather than borrowed. The diagnosis is honest and specific — Ranch was Heinz's worst-performing product in close to a decade — which gives the strategy a real business problem to solve. The leap that a celebrity tabloid moment alone justifies a structural relaunch is presented narratively, not data-backed.

execution.rationale: The build compresses product, packaging, distribution and PR into a 24-hour real-time launch — limited-edition bottles online plus 600+ store listings, followed by conversion to a permanent Walmart SKU once demand was proven. The orchestration is single-channel-heavy: social-first PR riding the Taylor Swift / Travis Kelce news cycle, with national US press (NYT, Washington Post, CNN, WSJ, TIME, Forbes, Fortune, ESQUIRE, Vanity Fair, Mashable) as the amplification layer.

impact.rationale: The '+$160M in sales' is the right business outcome for a SKU revival and is given as an absolute number, which is the strongest part of the case. The '320% above forecast' and '5x more than launch' percentages are unanchored — the absolute forecast and launch volumes are not disclosed, so the lift is directional rather than auditable. '6 billion most-earned impressions in brand history' is an entrant-reported reach claim, not a business outcome, and 'most-earned in brand history' is a superlative without a comparison baseline. The press list is broad and credibly signals earned conversation, but cannot stand in for the missing absolute baselines.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the 24-hour SKU revival and +$160M absolute sales number are genuinely strong, but the lift percentages are unanchored, the '6 billion impressions' is reach not outcome, and this is a single-SKU relaunch rather than a business-model change.

one_liner: An open library of medical illustrations on dark skin.
search_doc:
An open library of medical illustrations on dark skin.
Mechanisms: open-source-library-as-product, representation-as-product, industry-standard-rewrite, purpose-driven-infrastructure
Idea: We built the world's largest library of diverse medical illustrations across conditions that disproportionately affect people of color. Open-source and free to use for training and education, training future doctors and creating educational change in higher education. The platform is supported by UNDP and has 100+ illustrators worldwide.
Insight: Doctors cannot diagnose what they have never been shown — a centuries-old gap in medical illustration is directly producing worse outcomes for dark-skinned patients.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE BUSINESS TRANSFORMATION/BRONZE/Illustrate Change - Johnson&Johnson (Deloitte Digital, New York).jpeg

extracted.tagline: An utterly vital tool for patients and doctors.
extracted.context: For 2,000 years, medical illustrations have been used to educate doctors and patients. But in 2020 a study found that fewer than 5% of the body's of color illustrations shown in those texts. And less than 1% of medical artwork is people of color. This lack of representation forces patients and creates dangerous miscommunication and misdiagnosis that can be lethal. According to the European Academy of Dermatology and Venereology, dark skin patients are 4x more likely to die of skin cancer than fair skin people.
extracted.idea: We built the world's largest library of diverse medical illustrations across conditions that disproportionately affect people of color. Open-source and free to use for training and education, training future doctors and creating educational change in higher education. The platform is supported by UNDP and has 100+ illustrators worldwide.
execution: Open-source library of diverse medical illustrations covering 15+ conditions
Free for doctors, patients and medical educators
Initiative to bring lasting change to the healthcare system
Integrated into medical school curricula
Featured in The Wall Street Journal and an art gallery
metrics: Less than 5% of medical images show dark skin
Illustrate Change increased diverse medical images by more than 67% in one year

inferred.insight: Doctors cannot diagnose what they have never been shown — a centuries-old gap in medical illustration is directly producing worse outcomes for dark-skinned patients.
inferred.one_liner: An open library of medical illustrations on dark skin.
inferred.qualitative_summary: A pharma giant builds and open-sources the world's largest library of medical illustrations on diverse skin tones, lifting the share of diverse imagery in the field by more than 67% in a single year and earning industry, academic and UN endorsement.

visual.style_description: Editorial dark-backdrop poster: large hand-drawn medical illustrations of internal anatomy on dark skin form the hero strip across the top; a press-quote header from Fast Company sits above, and a dense thumbnail grid of conditions plus an institutional logo row run along the bottom under two bold metric callouts.

idea.rationale: Building an open-source library of medical illustrations on dark skin is a clear, on-brief idea that fixes a centuries-old visual gap in medical education with a real, usable asset. The originality is moderated by adjacent work in the diverse-skin / clinical-imagery space (e.g. Mind the Gap), so the idea reads more as scaling-and-institutionalising than as inventing a new mechanism.

strategy.rationale: Johnson & Johnson is one of the world's largest healthcare companies, positioned around 'health for humanity', so funding a piece of open medical-education infrastructure is mission-coherent rather than borrowed purpose. The diagnostic statistic — fewer than 5% of medical-textbook illustrations show dark skin, and the European Academy of Dermatology and Venereology finding that dark-skinned patients are 4x more likely to die of skin cancer — is specific, externally sourced and load-bearing for the case. The strategy is strong on social mission but light on the J&J commercial diagnosis (which product portfolio or clinician audience this reshapes), which is a soft spot in a business-transformation frame.

execution.rationale: The build is a free, open-source illustration library covering 15+ conditions, populated by 100+ illustrators worldwide, with curriculum integration into medical schools and UNDP partnership as the institutional layer. Amplification combines academic endorsement (Harvard, McGill, Quinnipiac), business / design press (Fast Company, Forbes, Wired, ADWEEK), and a Wall Street Journal feature plus a gallery exhibit. The phasing and the actual adoption-into-curriculum mechanics are described at a high level rather than in operational detail.

impact.rationale: The headline '+67% increase in diverse medical images in one year' is a percentage without a stated absolute base — 67% of what starting count, in which corpus? — which the brief flags as a soft impact claim. The '<5% of medical images show dark skin' is context, not outcome. No downstream clinical metric (diagnostic-accuracy lift, training-outcome change, patient-outcome shift) is reported, which is the actual business / mission outcome the platform is supposed to drive. The institutional roll-call and press list are credible earned-conversation signals but unaudited, and cannot substitute for the missing clinical or commercial outcome.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the open-source library is a tangible, mission-fit asset, but the impact case rests on a percentage with no absolute base and no downstream clinical or business outcome to show that the gap actually narrowed in practice.

one_liner: Small shops become the EV charging network.
search_doc:
Small shops become the EV charging network.
Mechanisms: business-model-pivot, peer-to-peer-network-as-product, rural-infrastructure-build, platform-extension-b2b
Idea: Plug Inn for Business is a B2B solution to connect small businesses with EV travelers. Through the app, small businesses can install and charge, advertise their business, and attract more customers. Reinforcing sustainable tourism and special deals. Over 83,000 users plug their cars while they eat, shop, sleep, or break out their hair in small, charming villages all over France. Plug Inn for Business pays small businesses back on the map by supporting and expanding sustainable tourism, and positions Renault as the leader of the electric transition.
Insight: EV charging in rural France is a missing-infrastructure problem — and small businesses already have the parking lots, the electricity and the need for more visitors.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE BUSINESS TRANSFORMATION/SILVER/Plug-Inn For Business - Renault (Publicis Conseil Paris).webp

extracted.tagline: Renault presents Plug Inn for business.
extracted.context: Renault, the leader in electric vehicles in France, launched Plug Inn, an app for people to charge their cars in remote locations that became the Airbnb for plugs. In 2024, Renault wanted to expand sustainable tourism, and it was the perfect opportunity to do more for EV drivers, small villages, and especially small businesses.
extracted.idea: Plug Inn for Business is a B2B solution to connect small businesses with EV travelers. Through the app, small businesses can install and charge, advertise their business, and attract more customers. Reinforcing sustainable tourism and special deals. Over 83,000 users plug their cars while they eat, shop, sleep, or break out their hair in small, charming villages all over France. Plug Inn for Business pays small businesses back on the map by supporting and expanding sustainable tourism, and positions Renault as the leader of the electric transition.
execution: B2B extension of the Plug Inn app for small businesses
Small businesses install and list EV chargers via the app
In-app advertising and special-deal channel for hosts
Promotes sustainable tourism in rural villages

inferred.insight: EV charging in rural France is a missing-infrastructure problem — and small businesses already have the parking lots, the electricity and the need for more visitors.
inferred.one_liner: Small shops become the EV charging network.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Renault converts its consumer Plug Inn app into a B2B charging-host platform that turns rural small businesses into the EV infrastructure, signing up 83,000 users and reframing the brand as a driver of sustainable rural tourism.

visual.style_description: Warm cinematic portrait of an elderly French shopkeeper in a hat inside his rustic store, with a giant smartphone showing the Plug Inn for Business app overlaid as the hero device; bottom strip shows charging-in-village execution thumbnails and one large white 'users' metric callout on the right.

idea.rationale: Extending the consumer 'Airbnb for EV plugs' into a B2B platform that turns rural shops, cafes and inns into the charging network is a coherent platform-extension idea that solves a real infrastructure gap. The originality sits in the B2B framing — the small-business host becomes the charging node — rather than in inventing the underlying peer-to-peer model, which already existed in the consumer Plug Inn.

strategy.rationale: Renault is the historic French national carmaker now positioned as 'leader of the electric transition' in France, and routing EV charging through rural small businesses ties its EV roadmap to its rural-dealer heritage, making the mission fit credible. The diagnostic — that rural France lacks public chargers and that small businesses already own the parking, the electricity and the appetite for more foot traffic — is observably true of the French countryside. The board does not cite specific rural-charging-gap statistics or small-business uptake data, leaving the otherwise strong insight unsourced.

execution.rationale: The build is a B2B layer on top of the existing Plug Inn app — host onboarding, in-app advertising for the small business, special-deal channel for EV travellers — distributed through the consumer app's user base and pitched as a sustainable-tourism play in rural villages. The phasing, partnership stack and amplification strategy beyond the app extension itself are not articulated on the board, which is a documentation gap rather than a craft one.

impact.rationale: The single disclosed metric, '83,000 users', is ambiguous — it likely refers to total consumer Plug Inn app users rather than B2B small-business hosts onboarded, which would be the load-bearing number for a B2B-platform pivot. No host count, no charging-session volume, no Renault EV-sales or share lift, and no business-press validation are shown, so the business-outcome side of a Creative Business Transformation case is conspicuously thin. The rural-tourism and small-business support narrative is plausible but quantitatively unproven on the board.

why_it_won.verdict: Silver because the platform-extension idea is on-brand and on-brief, but the proof set is a single ambiguous '83,000 users' number with no host count, no EV-sales lift and no business-press validation.

one_liner: Philips e-commerce now sells only refurbished products.
search_doc:
Philips e-commerce now sells only refurbished products.
Mechanisms: business-model-pivot, ecommerce-platform-overhaul, sustainability-as-product, category-stigma-reversal
Idea: Philips creates Better Than New: Refurb Editions, a range consisting of only returned and refurbished products. They are pristine condition, at lower prices, with a longer warranty. They overhauled the e-commerce platform globally to promote these exclusively over new products. On Earth Day, Philips stopped making new products and blacked their website front before relaunching it as a refurb-only store. They also priced refurbished gifts as an AB-forbidden test: turning a durable landfill into an intrusive digital store in which you could purchase them.
Insight: Consumers will buy refurbished electronics if the brand itself signals they are equal or better than new, removing the stigma of second-hand.

extracted.tagline: Turning an entire e-comm platform around to only sell refurbished products instead of new.
extracted.context: 16 million unwanted gifts end up in landfills every year. Global companies often encourage the purchase of new products, since it is more profitable than restocking, repackaging and remapping old products.
extracted.idea: Philips creates Better Than New: Refurb Editions, a range consisting of only returned and refurbished products. They are pristine condition, at lower prices, with a longer warranty. They overhauled the e-commerce platform globally to promote these exclusively over new products. On Earth Day, Philips stopped making new products and blacked their website front before relaunching it as a refurb-only store. They also priced refurbished gifts as an AB-forbidden test: turning a durable landfill into an intrusive digital store in which you could purchase them.
execution: Refurbished-only e-commerce store front
On Earth Day, the brand blacked out and relaunched the site selling only refurbished products
Refurbished product detail pages with longer warranty messaging
Press and PR coverage of the platform transformation

inferred.insight: Consumers will buy refurbished electronics if the brand itself signals they are equal or better than new, removing the stigma of second-hand.
inferred.one_liner: Philips e-commerce now sells only refurbished products.
inferred.qualitative_summary: A global brand reorients its direct-to-consumer e-commerce away from new units toward refurbished ones, delivering 185 tons of e-waste avoided, 277 tons of CO2 avoided and 52,000 refurbished products sold while earning major business and tech press coverage.

visual.style_description: Bold magenta-pink board with large white sans-serif headline 'BETTER THAN NEW'; hero shot of a refurbished Lumea IPL hair-removal device wrapped in a pink 'REFURBISHED' band on a tablet e-commerce page; three large blue metric callouts on the right; dense pink body text columns below.

idea.rationale: Flipping a global manufacturer's direct-to-consumer storefront from new-only to refurbished-only is a genuine business-model inversion, not a campaign idea dressed as transformation. The reframe redefines what 'Philips e-commerce' sells, which makes the work native to the Creative Business Transformation category.

strategy.rationale: Philips is a 130-year-old Dutch consumer-electronics multinational whose 'Better Lives' / circular-economy commitments make a refurb-only storefront an extension of stated corporate mission rather than borrowed purpose. The diagnostic — that manufacturers profit more from new stock than from restocking and refurbishing returns — is a structural truth of consumer electronics, anchored on the board by the '16 million unwanted gifts in landfills' frame. The insight that consumers accept refurbished only when the brand itself signals parity is plausible and consistent with category research, though the board states it as self-evident rather than data-backing it.

execution.rationale: The shift is engineered into the actual e-commerce stack — refurbished SKUs given their own product detail pages, longer-warranty messaging and an Earth Day blackout-then-relaunch moment that turns the platform overhaul into a press event. Channels are right-sized: owned e-commerce as the product, earned press (WIRED, BBC, NYT, DIE WELT) as the amplification layer, with the refurb assortment carried globally rather than piloted in a single market.

impact.rationale: The headline numbers are tied to absolute bases — 52,000 refurbished units sold, 185 tons of e-waste avoided, 277 tons of CO2 avoided — which is exactly the kind of business-and-environmental outcome a refurbishment pivot is meant to produce, and avoids the percentages-without-base failure mode. Press coverage in WIRED, BBC, NYT, The German Times and DIE WELT is entrant-reported but the breadth is a credible signal of earned business-press conversation rather than ad-trade chatter. What is missing is a multi-year run-rate or a share-of-total-Philips-revenue figure that would prove durable model change rather than launch-year lift.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins the Grand Prix because the brand actually changed what its e-commerce sells — a refurbished-only storefront with absolute tonnage and unit outcomes, not a comms campaign about sustainability.

one_liner: An online store that lends to the unbanked on trust.
search_doc:
An online store that lends to the unbanked on trust.
Mechanisms: business-model-pivot, financial-inclusion-platform, ecommerce-as-credit-engine, informal-economy-formalisation
Idea: The problem is that those stores just have the capacity to lend to a few neighbors, so we have created a larger one that can use the same system to lend to an entire country. WeCapital, seeking financial inclusion in Mexico, launched: the e-commerce of trust. An online store where unbanked Mexicans can request credit to fund the lives in neighborhood stores located in Cards, Acapulco and use only one platform: Trust.
Insight: This credit system based on trust has been the only financial support for millions of unbanked families for years.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE BUSINESS TRANSFORMATION/BRONZE/The E-Commerce Of Trust - WeCapital (DDB Mexico).jpg

extracted.context: In Mexico there are more than 38 million unbanked people, who do not have access to any type of credit. The only place where they are seen is in neighborhood stores, where they receive small food credits with just the promise of paying at the end of the month.
extracted.insight: This credit system based on trust has been the only financial support for millions of unbanked families for years.
extracted.idea: The problem is that those stores just have the capacity to lend to a few neighbors, so we have created a larger one that can use the same system to lend to an entire country. WeCapital, seeking financial inclusion in Mexico, launched: the e-commerce of trust. An online store where unbanked Mexicans can request credit to fund the lives in neighborhood stores located in Cards, Acapulco and use only one platform: Trust.
execution: Online e-commerce platform that lends to unbanked Mexicans on the neighborhood-store trust model
Loans of products tracked through the platform
Operating with neighborhood store networks
Awareness film and digital case rollout

inferred.one_liner: An online store that lends to the unbanked on trust.
inferred.qualitative_summary: WeCapital re-codes the neighbourhood-store trust-credit ritual into a scalable e-commerce platform that lends to unbanked Mexicans nationwide, reaching tens of thousands of families and earning Forbes and Guardian coverage.

visual.style_description: Hero portrait of a Mexican mother and two daughters facing the camera in a kitchen, with translucent e-commerce UI overlays (basket, price, product, trust icon) floating around them as if the store had become a website; left column holds context/idea body copy, four icon-labelled platform pillars sit centrally, and an execution thumbnail row plus press logo wall anchor the bottom.

idea.rationale: Codifying the neighbourhood-store trust-credit ritual into a scalable e-commerce platform that lends to unbanked Mexicans is a credible business-model invention — it is the product, not a campaign about a product. The mechanism leans on a well-known financial-inclusion pattern (group lending, store-based credit) and its originality is in the e-commerce framing rather than the underlying trust model.

strategy.rationale: WeCapital is a Mexican fintech positioning itself around financial inclusion for the unbanked, so a trust-credit e-commerce platform for unbanked Mexicans is exact mission-fit and not borrowed purpose. The diagnostic — that more than 38 million Mexicans are unbanked and rely on neighbourhood-store informal credit — is a real, documented characteristic of the Mexican retail economy. The insight that the informal trust-credit ritual can be re-coded as a digital platform is plausible but the board does not back it with default-rate or repayment-rate data that would prove the mechanism actually scales beyond the corner-store context.

execution.rationale: The build combines an online platform, partnerships with neighbourhood-store networks as the credit-trust anchor, a product-lending model rather than cash, and a launch film plus digital case rollout. Channels are reasonably matched to the target — leveraging existing store relationships as the credibility layer — but the board's narrative is partially unclear (city locations and platform mechanics are stated awkwardly) and the partnership architecture is not fully laid out.

impact.rationale: The disclosed metrics are absolute counts ('72,000 unofficial supplier', '8,500 mothers received', '15,000 schoolers') but the labels are ambiguous and partially unreadable on the board, which makes them hard to load-bear. No repayment rate, default rate, total credit disbursed, or share of the 38-million-unbanked target reached is shown, which are the load-bearing outcomes for a financial-inclusion platform. Press coverage is mixed in quality — Forbes, The Guardian and AdAge are credible business-press signals, but several other listed outlets appear garbled and are entrant-reported in either case.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the trust-credit e-commerce idea is mission-fit and category-native as a business model, but the impact set is a small string of ambiguous absolute counts with no repayment, default or credit-volume data to prove the model actually works at scale.
